citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 512: Categorical Eligibility
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 512: Categorical Eligibility

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 512: Categorical Eligibility

body:
Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) Handbook

Chapter 512: Categorical Eligibility
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 512: Categorical Eligibility 1
heading: 512 Title

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 512: Categorical Eligibility

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.1 General Policy
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.1 General Policy

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.1 General Policy

body:
Categorical eligibility applies to households, not individuals. Household composition must be determined before determining categorical eligibility. Separate household status is not granted to an individual household member because of categorical eligibility.

A categorically eligible household does not have to meet the resource limit, the gross income limit or net income limits .

7 CFR § 273.2(j)(2)

Categorically eligible households for Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) are households containing all cash a ssistance or Supplemental Security Income (SSI) recipients.

A recipient is an individual who:

Is receiving cash assistance - Temporary Assitance for Needy Families ( TANF) or State Blind Pension ( SBP ), or SSI benefits).

Is authorized for cash assistance or SSI but has not yet received payment;

Is eligible for cash assistance or SSI but has had benefits suspended or recouped; or

Is eligible for cash assistance of less than $5 a month and therefore not receiving payment.

7 CFR § 273.2(j)

Expanded categorically eligible households include those that:

do not have any elderly or disabled household members and have income at or below 200 percent of the Federal Poverty Income Guidelines (FPIGs).

have an elderly or disabled member and income at or below 200 percent of the FPIGs .

Expanded categorically eligible households receive the PUB 567 brochure Help for Pennsylvanians in Need . These households must meet the gross income within the limits discussed above.

NOTE: There is no gross income test applied in the system for elderly or disabled households. Elderly or disabled households with income exceed ing 200 percent of the FPIGs (though not categorically eligible) may remain potentially eligible if they pass the net test. Many households may qualify for the minimum benefit.

A household does not qualify for categorical eligibility or expanded categorical eligibility if a member of the household is disqualified for:

7 CFR § 273.2(j)(2)(vii)

Intentional program violation

Failure to comply with semiannual reporting (SAR) requirements

Failure to comply with employment and training requirements. This applies to head of household only.

A violation of a condition of probation or parole under a federal or state law.

The following individuals are not members of a SNAP household:

7 CFR § 273.2(j)(2)(ix)

Ineligible noncitizens

Ineligible students

Institutionalized individuals, unless the facility meets an exemption (See Chapter 511 Living Arrangements )

Household Examples:

Example 1: Mr . and Mrs. B, ages 63 and 60 respectively, apply for SNAP. The CAO provides them with Pub 567, Help for Pennsylvanians in Need. Their gross monthly income is $1550, and their countable resources are $3200. The Bs are eligible for SNAP under expanded categorical eligibility criteria. Their income is below 200 percent of FPIGs and there is no resource limit for the household.

Example 2: The C family of four applies for SNAP. The household is comprised of a 67-year-old individual , the adult child of the 67-year-old, the adult child’s spouse and their child. The CAO provides them with PUB 567 , Help for Pennsylvanians in Need . Monthly gross income of $2460 is from SSI and wages. Resources are $4500. The C family is eligible for SNAP under expanded categorical eligibility criteria. Their income is below 200 percent of FPIGs and there is no resource limit for the household.

Example 3: Mr. and Mrs. N apply for SNAP for themselves and a 9-year-old child. They are not elderly or disabled. The CAO provides them with PUB 567, Help for Pennsylvanians in Need . The N family reports gross monthly income of $2600 and countable resources of $7000. The N family is eligible for SNAP under expanded categorical eligibility criteria. Their income is below 200 percent of FPIGS and there is no resource limit for the household.

Example 4: Mr . and Mrs. A apply for SNAP for themselves and one child. The CAO gives them PUB 567, Help for Pennsylvanians in Need . The CAO reviews the case file and discovers that Mr. A is still within the 12- month period of ineligibility for an intentional program violation (IPV). The family is ineligible for SNAP under expanded categorical eligibility criteria due to the IPV. Mrs. A and their child may receive SNAP if income is below 130 percent of FPIGs and resources do not exceed $2250.

Updated March 13, 2026, replacing September 6, 2022
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.1 General Policy 1
heading: 512.1 General Policy

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.1 General Policy

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.2 The Application Process
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.2 The Application Process

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.2 The Application Process

body:
Categorically eligible households and expanded categorically eligible households are certified using the same requirements applied to any other Supplemental Nutrition Assitance Program (SNAP) household .

7 CFR § 273.2(j)
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.2 The Application Process 1
heading: 512.2 The Application Process

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.2 The Application Process

body:
If questionable, the County Assitance Office (CAO) must verify that households:

7 CFR § 273.2(j)(1)

Have only members who receive Temporary Assitance for Needy Families (TANF) cash assistance, Family Works ( FW ), or Supplemental Security Income ( SSI) benefits (categorically eligible households only);

Meet all household requirements in Chapter 510 - Households ;

Include all individuals who purchase and prepare food together in one SNAP household, even if they are separate units for cash assistance or SSI; and

Do not include disqualified household members .

The CAO will not verify the following for categorically eligible households:

7 CFR § 273.2(j)(2)

Resources

That household income is within gross and net income limits

Social Security number information

Sponsored noncitizen information

Residency

The CAO will verify the following for expanded categorically eligible households:

That gross income is at or below 200 percent of the FPIGs for households without an elderly or disabled member

That gross income is at or below 200 percent of the FPIGs for households having an elderly or disabled member

Any other eligibility factors where verification is required by the SNAP program as outlined throughout the SNAP handbook.
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.2 The Application Process 2
heading: 512.21 Verification

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.2 The Application Process

body:
The CAO will postpone denial of benefits for a potential categorically eligible household until the 30th day in case the household is found eligible for cash assistance or SSI benefits.

7 CFR § 273.2(j)(1)(iv)

The CAO will not postpone the eligibility decision if:

The household is entitled to expedited service ; or

It appears that the cash assistance or SSI application will not be approved by the 30th day and the household will not be categorically eligible.

If the CAO postpones the SNAP eligibility decision, and cash assistance or SSI eligibility is still pending on the 30th day, the CAO will:

Determine the household's eligibility for SNAP on the 30th day.

Inform the ineligible household with the Notice to Applicant ( PA 162 ) of their ineligibility and that it must tell the CAO if its cash assistance or SSI benefits are approved.

Ensure the denied application of a potential categorically eligible household is easily retrievable should cash assistance or SSI benefits be approved.

F or households that are determined categorically eligible before the SNAP eligibility determination, the CAO will:

Authorize benefits back to the date of the SNAP application.

For households that are determined categorically eligible after the SNAP benefits are denied, the CAO will:

Authorize benefits back to the date of the SNAP application or the effective date of the cash assistance or SSI, whichever is later

Not require another interview unless there are significant changes.

The CAO will update the original application from available information or through mail or phone contact with the household or authorized representative . The CAO will ask the household member or authorized representative to initial any changes and sign the updated application again.

The CAO must reevaluate the original application at the household’s request or if the CAO learns that the household is eligible for cash assistance or SSI.

Once the cash assistance application is approved and a household is found categorically eligible, the CAO will:

Use the initial cash assistance payment to calculate the SNAP benefit for that month if it is reasonably sure what the amount and payment date will be.

Disregard any part of the initial cash assistance payment intended to cover a previous month.

Send a Notice to Applicant ( PA 162 ) to the household when SNAP eligibility is determined, stating that receipt of cash assistance may reduce, suspend, or end benefits.

Treat the initial cash assistance payment as a change in circumstances if the amount and receipt date of the first cash assistance payment can not be reasonably anticipated when SNAP eligibility is determined.

NOTE: The CAO is not required to send an Advance Notice of Adverse Action if it notified the household in advance that a cash assistance grant might reduce, suspend, or end its SNAP benefits.

For expanded categorically eligible households, the CAO will follow processing procedures as it would with any other SNAP household. (See Chapter 504 ).

Updated March 13, 2026, replacing September 6, 2022
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.2 The Application Process 3
heading: 512.22 Delayed Processing

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.2 The Application Process

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.3 Issuing Benefits
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.3 Issuing Benefits

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.3 Issuing Benefits

body:
The CAO will calculate net income for all categorically eligible and expanded categorically eligible households .

7 CFR § 273.10 (e)(2)

For one- and two-person categorically eligible households, the CAO must issue a minimum $24 benefit, even if the Basis of Issuance Table shows no entitlement amount. (See Chapter 568, Appendix B . )

There is no minimum benefit for households with three or more persons. The CAO must issue the amount shown in the Basis of Issuance Tables.

NOTE: Categorically eligible households with three or more members may be entitled to benefits of at least $2. If the computation results in a benefit of $1, $3, or $5, round it up to $2, $4, or $6, respectively.

Exception: The benefit is prorated according to the proration table in the first application month. See Chapter 568, Appendix C .
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.3 Issuing Benefits 1
heading: 512.3 Issuing Benefits

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.3 Issuing Benefits

body:
Some categorically eligible and expanded categorically eligible households of three or more persons will not be eligible for benefits because their net income is over the limit for which a benefit may be issued. The system will generate the appropriate notice using reason code 322.

Updated March 13, 2026, replacing September 9, 2025
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.3 Issuing Benefits 2
heading: 512.31 Ineligibility for Benefits

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.3 Issuing Benefits

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.4 Termination of Categorical Eligibility
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.4 Termination of Categorical Eligibility

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.4 Termination of Categorical Eligibility

body:
The household may no longer be categorically eligible when cash assistance or Supplemental Security Income ( SSI) benefits end during the certification period .

The household may no longer qualify for SNAP under expanded categorical eligibility criteria when income exceeds the specified limits or when the household has a disqualified member (See Chapter 512.1 for expanded categorical eligibility income limits and disqualification reasons).

The County Assitance Office ( CAO) will review all SNAP eligibility requirements to see whether the household remains eligible.

The CAO will send the appropriate notice informing the household of any change in eligibility or benefit level.

7 CFR § 273.2(j)

Updated March 13, 2026, replacing July 30, 2015
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.4 Termination of Categorical Eligibility 1
heading: 512.4 T ermination of Categorical or Expanded Categorical Eligibility

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 512.4 Termination of Categorical Eligibility

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 513: Strikers
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 513: Strikers

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 513: Strikers

body:
Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) Handbook

Chapter 513: Strikers
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 513: Strikers 1
heading: 513 Tile

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 513: Strikers

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.1 General Policy
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.1 General Policy

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.1 General Policy

body:
A household with a striker is not eligible for SNAP benefits unless the household was eligible the day before the strike began and is currently eligible.

7 CFR § 273.1(e)

The CAO must decide whether an applicant household was eligible before the strike by using the day before the strike as the application date and determining eligibility as if the strike did not occur.

7 CFR § 273.1(e)(1)

For currently certified households, the benefit amount may not be increased if the striker’s income has decreased because of the strike.

7 CFR § 273.1(e)(2)

Reviewed May 9, 2024 , replacing March 1, 2012

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.2 Who Is a Striker
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.2 Who Is a Striker

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.2 Who Is a Striker

body:
Persons considered strikers for SNAP purposes include, but are not limited to:

A person involved in a planned work slowdown, stoppage or interruption of work by employees, including work stoppages when a collective bargaining agreement expires

An employee who participates in the strike out of sympathy

An employee who is a nonworking member of the bargaining unit on strike but is on leave at the time of the strike, even if the employee is not a union member and regardless of whether the employee is afraid to cross the picket line

An individual who gets another job while on strike but does not resign from the struck company

A person who does not go back to work when the union calls off the strike and tells the strikers to return to work

An employee of a struck company that has filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy

NOTE: A person is considered a striker until he or she goes back to work, is fired, or quits.

7 CFR § 273.1(e)

Reviewed May 9, 2024, replacing March 1, 2012
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.2 Who Is a Striker 1
heading: 513.2 Who Is a Striker

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.2 Who Is a Striker

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.3 Who Is Not a Striker
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.3 Who Is Not a Striker

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.3 Who Is Not a Striker

body:
Individuals not considered strikers for the purposes of deciding SNAP eligibility include, but are not limited to:

An employee whose workplace is closed because of a lockout

NOTE: If the union and the company disagree as to whether a work stoppage is a strike or a lockout, see Section 513.6 . The fact that a person gets unemployment compensation does not mean that the person is a nonstriking employee for SNAP.

An employee who is not on strike but cannot work as a result of the strike

Example : A truck driver who cannot work because striking press workers prevent newspapers from being printed

An employee who is not part of the bargaining unit on strike but does not want to cross the picket line for fear of personal injury or death

Employees who are fired or permanently replaced while on strike

Employees whose employer will not allow them to return to their old jobs but offers them different jobs instead

NOTE: This provision does not apply to federal government or political subdivision employees who are dismissed from the job because of participation in a strike. These employees are considered to have voluntarily quit without good cause .

An employee who officially resigns from the job while on strike

Others who are not on strike but are affected by a strike (e.g., A self employed individual such as an independent long-distance trucker

A person who is exempt from work registration, if:

the person was exempt the day before the strike, and

the individual was exempt for a reason other than being employed..

An employee who is a member of the bargaining unit and is working

An employee who is not part of the bargaining unit and is laid off, furloughed, or told by the company that there are no available jobs because of the strike

An employee who was laid off when the strike began

An employee who was locked out by the company the day before the strike

7 CFR § 273.1(e)

Reviewed May 9, 2024, replacing March1, 2012

body:
The CAO will compute gross income as follows:

Compare the striker’s monthly income before the strike to the striker's current income.

Choose the larger amount (from #1) and add it to the current income of the nonstriking household members.

7 CFR § 273.1(e)(2)

Reviewed May 9, 2024, replacing March 1, 2012

body:
The CAO must compute net income as follows:

Allow deductions for the current month, even when using the striker’s pre-strike income.

Allow the earned income deduction for all household members, including the striker.

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.6 Determining the Status of a Work Stoppage
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.6 Determining the Status of a Work Stoppage

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 513.6 Determining the Status of a Work Stoppage

body:
If the company and the union disagree about whether a work stoppage is a strike or a lockout , the CAO will provide its area manager with the following information:

The name and address of the company

The names and telephone numbers of company and union representatives

The date the work stoppage began

Demographic information about an applicant

The area manager will forward this information to the Bureau of Policy.

The Bureau of Policy will:

Contact the company and union representatives for their statements, and

Contact the PA Department of Labor and Industry to ask whether it has issued an official determination and if unemployment compensation benefits are authorized.

NOTE: The PA Department of Labor and Industry’s Office of Unemployment Compensation Programs decides whether the situation is a strike or a lockout. DHS must accept this decision.

If a determination is not made at the state level, FNS will determine whether the situation is a strike or a lockout.

The area manager will notify the CAO of the decision so an appropriate eligibility decision can be made.

Reviewed May 9, 2024, replacing March 1, 2012

heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 514: Students

body:
Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) Handbook

Chapter 514: Students
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 514: Students 1
heading: 514 Title

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 514: Students

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.1 General Policy
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.1 General Policy

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.1 General Policy

body:
PFS-22082-514 , Countability of In-Kind and Unpaid Work for Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) Student Eligibility (Published March 18, 2025)

A student at an institution of higher education (IHE) must meet the an exemption as outlined in 514.2 Eligible Students to be a household member. A student who does not meet these eligibility requirements is ineligible to participate in SNAP.

7 CFR § 273.5(b)

NOTE: Income and resources of ineligible students are not counted when determining a household’s eligibility and benefit amount unless the student is also an ineligible non-citizen, an ineligible ABAWD, or disqualified for failure to get or verify an SSN (see 555.3 ).

7 CFR § 273.11(d)(1)

5 14.11 Who Is a Student?

The CAO must consider three criteria when determining if an individual is a student of higher education for SNAP eligibility purposes: enrollment status, the type of school, and the type of curriculum.

To be considered a student, an individual must be enrolled at least half-time in an IHE in a curriculum or program that normally requires a high school diploma or equivalent degree prior to admission.

Enrollment Status

A student must be enrolled at least half-time as determined by the school. Individuals enrolled less than half-time do not have to meet a student exemption to be eligible for SNAP.

7 CFR § 273.5(a)

Type of School

An IHE is defined as a school that normally requires a high school diploma or equivalent degree prior to enrollment. IHEs can include business schools, technical schools, trade schools, vocational schools, or colleges and universities.

7 CFR § 273.5(a)

Type of Curriculum

At a business, technical, trade, or vocational school, only individuals enrolled in curricula that require a high school diploma or equivalent degree prior to enrollment are considered to be enrolled in an IHE. Individuals enrolled in curricula or programs within those schools that do not require a high school diploma or equivalent degree prior to enrollment are not considered to be enrolled in an IHE.

At a college or university, if an individual is enrolled in a program that is part of the regular curriculum or degree program, they are considered enrolled in an IHE whether or not they were required to have a high school diploma or equivalent degree. If the individual is enrolled in a special program that is not part of the regular curriculum (such as remedial education, continuing or community education, professional development, classes for English language learners, or workforce development/training programs), then they are not considered to be enrolled in an IHE.

7 CFR § 273.5(a)

Updated April 24, 2023, replacing March 1, 2012
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.1 General Policy 1
heading: 514.1 General Policy

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.1 General Policy

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.2 Eligible Students
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.2 Eligible Students

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.2 Eligible Students

body:
Federal Exemptions

To be included in the SNAP household , a student must meet at least one of the following criteria:

Attending high school, which is not an IHE

Attending a school or training program that is not an institution of higher education

Enrolled in an institution of higher education less than half-time, as determined by the institution

7 CFR § 273.5(a)

Under age 18 or

Age 50 or older

7 CFR § 273.5(b)(1)

Physically or mentally unfit

NOTE: Verification is required when the unfitness is not evident to the CAO . Verification may be, but is not limited to, either proof that the student gets a disability benefit or a statement from a physician or a licensed or certified psychologist.

7 CFR § 273.5(b)(2)

Responsible for the care of a dependent household member under age 6

7 CFR § 273.5(b)(8)

Responsible for the care of a dependent household member older than 5 but under age 12 if adequate child care is not available for the student to attend class and satisfy the 20-hour weekly work requirement or participate in a state or federally financed work-study program during the regular school year

7 CFR § 273.5(b)(9)

Enrolled full-time in an institution of higher education, as determined by the institution, and a single parent (or a single caretaker, if no parent is present) responsible for the care of a dependent child under age 12, regardless of the availability of adequate child care

7 CFR § 273.5(b)(10)

Participating in an on-the-job-training program

7 CFR § 273.5(b)(7)

Working 20 hours a week or self-employed with weekly earnings equal to at least 20 hours times the federal minimum hourly wage or participating in a state or federally financed work-study program during the regular school year

7 CFR § 273.5(b)(5)

NOTE: Earnings do not have to be countable for a student to be eligible. For example, a student with excluded earnings from a temporary census job may qualify as an eligible student.

NOTE: An unpaid internship does not fulfill the requirements for SNAP eligibility.

Participating in a work-study program under Title IV of the Higher Education Act of 1965

7 CFR § 273.5(b)(6)

NOTE: work study earnings are not countable as income.

Receiving TANF benefits

7 CFR § 273.5(b)(3)

Enrolled in an institution of higher education through or to comply with the requirements of any of the following:

A program under Title I of the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act (WIOA)

The Workforce Investment Act (WIA) program

The SNAP Employment and Training (E&T) program (see Chapter 535.1 )

The Trade Act of 1974 (Section 236) program

7 CFR § 273.5(b)(11)

Pennsylvania’s Expansion

If a student does not meet a Federal Exemption as listed above, to be included in the SNAP household , a student must meet at least one of the following criteria as part of Pennsylvania’s expansion to SNAP student eligibility:

Enrolled in one of Pennsylvania's community colleges at least half-time in a career or technical education program under the Perkins V program or in a course of study associated with a High Priority Occupation (HPO) as verified by the college.

CAOs may use the Community College Verification Form (PA 1959) to verify.

The CAO will use SNAP student code “13 – Placed in Inst of Higher Ed by JTPA, ETP, Sect 236 of the 1974 Trade Act or a State or Local E&T Prog approved by FNS” and narrate that the individual is eligible under the Perkins V program or a course of study associated with an HPO.

Students newly eligible for SNAP through this policy may not be referred to SNAP KEYS unless attending a school that is part of the demonstration project as described below. Students attending demonstration project schools should be encouraged to but are not required to participate in SNAP KEYS.

Name of Community College Refer to KEYS? Bucks County Community College no Butler County Community College yes Community College of Allegheny County yes Community College of Beaver County no Community College of Philadelphia yes Delaware County Community College no Erie County Community College no* Harrisburg Area Community College yes Lehigh Carbon Community College no Luzerne County Community College no Montgomery County Community College yes Northampton Community College no Pennsylvania Highlands Community College no Reading Area Community College no Westmoreland County Community College no

*Erie County Community College does not yet have a KEYS program.

7 CFR § 273.5(b)(11)(iv)

Enrolled in an IHE through or in compliance with a program operated by a state or local government which has been certified by the Bureau of Policy and Bureau of Employment Programs to serve students from low-income households and be equivalent to at least one allowable activity under SNAP E&T.

o For a list of all currently approved programs and more information about certifying new programs, see Appendix A .

o Use the Comparable Program Verification Form to verify enrollment status if questionable.
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.2 Eligible Students 1
heading: 514.2 Eligible Students

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.2 Eligible Students

body:
Students who are enrolled at least half-time in an institution of higher education and do not meet one of the above requirements are ineligible for SNAP.

Students who meet one of the requirements above and buy a meal plan that gives them more than half of their weekly meals are ineligible for SNAP.

“More than half” means a meal plan that provides 11 or more meals per week. Because meal plans vary among IHEs, the CAO should review each student’s circumstances and may need to ask the student for clarification if it is unclear how many meals per week they receive from a meal plan. The CAO may expand the review to meals per month if meals per week does not clearly determine eligibility. To do this, the CAO should count the number of meals received in a 4-week period. If the meal plan covers 43 or more of their 84 meals in a 4-week period, the student is considered ineligible.

7 CFR § 273.1(b)(7)(vi)

Example 1: Mary attends Millersville University and has the 150 Block meal plan, which includes 150 meal swipes per semester plus $250 flex dollars. 150 meals over a 15-week semester would be 10 meals per week, and $250 in flex would be about $16-17 per week. The CAO is not sure how the flex money is spent and asks Mary. Mary states she purchases one meal per month with the flex dollars and spends the rest on some snacks for studying. Using the 4-week calculation, this would be 10 meals per week times 4 weeks for the base of the plan plus 1 meal per month using flex for a total of 41 meals per month. This meal plan covers less than half of her 84 meals in a month , so her meal plan does not make her ineligible.

Example 2: Justin attends Mansfield University and has the 14 weekly with $250 Flex plan. Because this plan covers 11 or more meals per week, Justin is ineligible.

Example 3: Francisco attends Slippery Rock University and has the Semester 75 plan, which includes 75 meals per semester plus $100 flex dollars. 75 meals over a 15-week semester would be 5 meals per week and $100 in flex would be about $6-7 per week. This meal plan covers fewer than 11 meals per week, so Francisco’s meal plan does not make them ineligible.

The CAO should not count meals which a student receives irregularly through student-to-student donation programs like Swipe Out Hunger toward the 11 or more meals calculation.
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.2 Eligible Students 2
heading: 514.21 Ineligible Students

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.2 Eligible Students

body:
A student’s enrollment begins on the first day of the institution of higher education's term.

A student's enrollment ends upon graduation, suspension, expulsion, dropping out, or not intending to register for the next normal school term.

A student who was enrolled during the spring term and intends to enroll for the fall term is considered enrolled during summer vacation, even though he or she is not attending school. Summer school is not considered a normal school term.

Enrollment status continues when a student graduates from an institution of higher education and intends to enroll in a new course of study for the next normal school term.

7 CFR § 273.5(c)

Example: A student who graduates with their bachelor's degree in June and intends to enroll in graduate school in the fall is considered enrolled during summer vacation.

Updated August 30, 2023, replacing April 24, 2023

heading: 514.22 Enrollment Status

heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.3 Income of Eligible Students

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.3 Income of Eligible Students

body:
The CAO must count the income and resources not specifically excluded of an eligible student when determining eligibility and the benefit amount.

7 CFR § 273.8

7 CFR § 273.9(b)

An eligible student's income must be treated the same way as the income of other household members. See Chapter 550 .

7 CFR § 273.10(c)
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.3 Income of Eligible Students 1
heading: 514.3 Income of Eligible Students

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.3 Income of Eligible Students

body:
All educational assistance of an eligible student is excluded when determining eligibility and amount of SNAP benefits, regardless of the source of the educational assistance.

This exclusion includes any tuition account or college savings bond purchased under the Tuition Account Program and College Savings Bond Act , and work study earnings received under the Higher Education Act .

The funds and any interest accrued remain exempt as long as they are used for educational purposes. Funds used for any purpose other than educational expenses are treated as a resource . See Section 540.3 .

7 CFR § 273.9(c)(3)(v)

55 Pa. Code § 181.262
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.3 Income of Eligible Students 2
heading: 514.31 Excluded Educational Expenses

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.3 Income of Eligible Students

body:
Educational assistance provided to a third party on behalf of the household for living expenses shall be treated the same as educational assistance payable directly to the household. The CAO must exclude any educational assistance vendor payment made to a school or other third party, even if the payment is used for living expenses.

7 CFR § 273.9(c)(1)(iv)

Updated April 24, 2023, replacing March 1, 2012
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.3 Income of Eligible Students 3
heading: 514.32 Income from Vendor Payments for Educational Expenses

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.4 Verification of Student Income
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.4 Verification of Student Income

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 514.4 Verification of Student Income

body:
An ineligible student’s income is not counted when determining a household’s eligibility and benefit amount. Therefore, the CAO must not require a household to verify an ineligible student’s income.

An eligible student’s gross non-exempt income must be verified (see: Chapter 578.32 ).

Since educational assistance is exempt income, the CAO must not require verification of educational assistance, unless questionable. If a situation arises that requires further clarification, verification of educational assistance may include an award letter or statement from the school or from the grantor of the loan, grant, or scholarship.

Verification may be provided by the household , if the grantor or school refuses to provide the information.

7 CFR § 273.2(f)

Updated April 24, 2023 , replacing March 1, 2012

body:
Appendix A: Approved Programs That Increase Employability

Enrolled Through Programs That Increase Employability

TRIO Upward Bound

GEARUP

ELECT

Title II Adult Basic Education

Chafee Education and Training Grant

Enrolled in Compliance With Programs That Increase Employability

Act 101 Programs

Fostering Independence Tuition Waiver Program

TRIO Student Support Services (SSS) programs at the following IHEs only:

University of Pittsburgh Pennsylvania Western University (California, Clarion, and Edinboro)

TRIO McNair Scholars Program at the following IHE only: University of Pittsburgh

Kessler Scholars program at the following IHE only: University of Pittsburgh

Additional Programs That Establish a Student Exemption

TANF RESET

WIOA Title I

Bridge Programs and Remedial Programs

Certifying Additional Programs

The above list of programs is not intended to be exhaustive. In addition to the programs listed, there may be other local programs that meet all three criteria for certification. To qualify, a program must:

Primarily serve students from low-income households;

Be operated by a state or local government, or instrumentality thereof; and

Provide the equivalent of at least one allowable activity under SNAP E&T. (See SHB 535 Appendix A )

CAO Responsibilities

If a client believes a program in which they are participating would meet the above criteria, the CAO may ask for the contact information for a school official who can verify the program details. The CAO may also provide the SNAP College Program Checklist to the college or IHE. If the CAO receives a completed SNAP College Program Checklist from an IHE or receives an inquiry from an IHE about adding a specific program to the list of eligible programs, the CAO should forward all information and IHE contact details to RA-PWSNAPIHEElig@pa.gov .

College or IHE Responsibilities

Colleges should review the criteria above to be sure that the program they are asking about is potentially eligible. Colleges may submit their formal requests directly to RA-PWSNAPIHEElig@pa.gov using the SNAP College Program Checklist .

Headquarters Responsibilities

The Bureau of Policy and Bureau of Employment Programs will jointly certify programs which meet all three criteria, inform the CAO and the IHE of the final determination, and update this Appendix with any additional approved programs.

Updated March 4, 2025, replacing February 27, 2025

body:
Eligibility requirements for households of migrant or seasonal farm workers are different from those of other households in the following areas:

Application

Expedited service

Verification

Income computation

Work requirements

Resources

Income

Semiannual reporting (SAR)

Certification period

Proration

Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing August 20, 2008
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.1 General Policy 1
heading: 516.1 General Policy

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.1 General Policy
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
A destitute household with liquid resources of $100 or less has the right to get expedited service . Expedited service allows a household to get SNAP benefits faster than usual. All household members must be migrant or seasonal farm workers to be considered destitute.

7 CFR § 273.2(i)(1)(ii)

All expedited service rules in Chapter 506 apply to migrant or seasonal farm worker households that are entitled to expedited service. The CAO must make sure that households eligible for expedited service receive their first SNAP benefits within five calendar days of the application receipt date.

7 CFR § 273.10(e)(3)

A household is destitute if its only income during the month of application is:

From a terminated (ended) source, received before the application date;

Not more than $25 from a new source received within 10 days of the application; or

A combination of terminated and new sources of income.
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers 1
heading: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
The grower, not the crew chief, is the migrant or seasonal farm worker's source of income. A farm worker who travels with the same crew chief from one grower to another is considered to have moved from a terminated source of income.

7 CFR § 273.10(e)(3)(vi)

The crew chief is the source of income if the crew chief pays a migrant or seasonal farm worker for work done directly for the crew chief, such as bookkeeping or cooking.

If a seasonal farm worker changes job locations but still works for the same employer, the source has not changed. For example, if a worker picks apples for Knouse Fruitland in October and then goes to work in their processing factory in November, the household is not destitute. The source of income has not terminated.

The CAO must evaluate all income sources to determine destitution if the migrant or seasonal farm worker's household has more than one source of income.
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers 2
heading: 516.21 Identifying the Source of Income

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
A terminated source of income is income that stopped before the application date and is not expected again from the same source, no matter the amount of the terminated income.

7 CFR § 273.10(e)(3)(i)

The CAO must count the actual amount of any terminated income received in the month of application when determining eligibility and benefit amount.

Income is considered from a “terminated source” in these two ways:

The household receives income monthly or more frequently and does not expect income from the same source from the application date through the next month.

Example: A migrant farm worker reports at application on July 16 that work with a local grower ended in early July and that he got his last weekly pay on July 15. He will not be paid by his new grower until August. The only income for the month is from a terminated source, because he will get no further income from the first grower in July or August. The household is destitute.

The household receives income less often than monthly and does not expect income from the same source in the next payment month.

Example: Quarterly income last received in April would not normally be received again until July. If a payment is not expected in July, the income is from a terminated source, and the household is destitute in July.
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers 3
heading: 516.22 Destitution—Terminated Source of Income

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
If the migrant or seasonal farm worker will receive more than $25 from a new source of income within ten days after the application date, the household is not destitute.

7 CFR § 273.10(e)(3)(ii)

The CAO must not expect income if neither an amount over $25 nor receipt within ten days can be reasonably predicted.

Example: A migrant farm worker household applies on September 5. The only income expected for the application month is pay from a job with a new grower. Work began on September 1, and the first pay is expected on September 10. The CAO contacts the grower and verifies the pay date and a gross amount of $67. The household is not destitute. The CAO must determine whether the household meets other criteria for expedited service.

Income is from a new source if it is:

Received monthly or more frequently, and not more than $25 from that source was received in the 30 days before the application date.

Examples:

A migrant farm worker applies on April 10 and reports he will start work for a new employer the next week. The first pay date is April 23. The income is from a new source, because he received no money from the new employer in the past 30 days. The household is destitute, because the income is from a new source and is not expected to be over $25 within 10 days.

A farm worker applies on July 10 and reports that he began work for a new grower on June 24 and received $25 on July 1. He expects a second payment from the same grower on July 28. The farm worker is destitute.

Received less often than monthly, and not more than $25 from that source was received within the last normal payment interval.

Example: A household reports at application that it received quarterly income last January and will not receive it in April. It received no income since the January payment. The income is from a new source, and the farm worker's household is destitute.
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers 4
heading: 516.23 Destitution—New Source of Income

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
A travel expense paid by a new employer as reimbursement must not be counted when determining destitution. However, travel expenses given under written contract as a pay advance must count as income when determining destitution.

7 CFR § 273.10(e)(3)(v)

The contract may be a written agreement signed by both parties, or it may be a written statement from the employer addressed to the employee at the time of or before the worker receives the advance. The agreement must state that the advance will be deducted from future pay.
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers 5
heading: 516.231 Travel Expenses

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
A migrant or seasonal farm worker household may receive income from a terminated source before the application date and from a new source after the application date and still be considered destitute. (See Section 516.22 and Section 516.23 .)

7 CFR § 273.10(e)(3)(iii)

Example: A migrant household applying on July 10 is part of a crew that moved from one grower to another to harvest different crops. The household received its last pay from the previous grower on July 8 and will receive its first pay from the new grower on July 21. The household is destitute, because its only income for the application month is from both new and terminated sources and it will not receive any income from the new source within ten days after the application date.
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers 6
heading: 516.24 Destitution—Terminated and New Sources of Income

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
The CAO must count only income received between the first of the month and the application date when determining the benefit amount for a destitute migrant or seasonal farm worker.

7 CFR § 273.10(e)(3)(iv)

The CAO must disregard any income anticipated from a new source after the application date.

NOTE: This also applies if the farm worker meets destitution criteria for the first month of a new certification period.

The CAO must not average income for destitute migrant or seasonal farm worker households and must not count any income expected after the application date when calculating the first month's benefit.

Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing August 20, 2008
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers 7
heading: 516.25 Destitution—Income Computation

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.2 Expedited Service for Destitute Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.3 Application
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.3 Application

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.3 Application
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
The CAO must not require a migrant or seasonal farm worker to file an application with other crew members.

7 CFR § 273.2(c)(1)(2)

The CAO must not require the farm worker to get help or verification from the crew leader, employer, or potential employer.

The seasonal farm worker may choose an employer or crew leader as an AR . The CAO must make sure that:

7 CFR § 273.2(n)

The household ’s circumstances are correctly represented.

The household is receiving the correct benefit amount.

The AR is using the PA EBT ACCESS card properly.

The household freely requested the assistance of the AR.

Also see Chapter 505 .

Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing August 20, 2008
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.3 Application 1
heading: 516.3 Application

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.3 Application
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.4 Verification
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.4 Verification

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.4 Verification
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
All verification requirements of Chapter 578 apply to migrant or seasonal farm worker household applications except for the requirement to verify residence. The CAO may waive this requirement if verification cannot reasonably be done.

7 CFR § 273.2(f)

The CAO must verify the identity of the seasonal farm worker applicant. If documentary evidence is not available, the CAO must contact a collateral source.

7 CFR § 273.2(i)(4)(i)(A)

Examples of acceptable collateral sources include crew leaders, growers, or migrant service agencies (such as Pennsylvania Farm Worker Opportunities).
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.4 Verification 1
heading: 5 16.4 Verification

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.4 Verification
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
The CAO must make a reasonable effort to verify destitution, but only if the effort will not delay expedited benefits.

7 CFR § 273.2(i)(4)(i)(B)

The migrant or seasonal farm worker household must provide postponed verification from in-state sources before the CAO issues the second monthly benefit.

7 CFR § 273.2(i)(4)(iii)(B)

The migrant or seasonal farmer worker household must provide postponed verification from out-of-state sources before the CAO issues the third monthly benefit.

The CAO must complete data entry for issuing SNAP benefits for the second or third month on the first work day of the issuance month or within five calendar days after verification receipt, whichever is later.

Migrant or seasonal farm worker households are entitled to postpone out-of-state verification only once per season.

If a household applying for expedited benefits has already postponed this verification during the current season, the CAO must grant a postponement of out-of-state verification only for the first month’s benefits and not for the second month.

Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing August 20, 2008
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.4 Verification 2
heading: 516.41 Verification for Expedited Service

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.4 Verification
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.5 Work Requirements
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.5 Work Requirements

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.5 Work Requirements
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
A migrant or seasonal farm worker is exempt from work requirements if he or she is under contract to, or has an agreement with, an employer or crew chief to begin employment within 30 days. The CAO must assign employment/training exemption code 21 to migrant or seasonal farm workers who meet this exemption.

7 CFR § 273.7(b)(1)(vii)

A migrant or seasonal farm worker who moves from job to job in a typical migrant farm labor pattern has not voluntarily quit employment. Even though the farm worker does not start work with a new crew or grower right away, the CAO must determine that good cause existed and must not apply the voluntary quit provisions of Chapter 535 .

7 CFR § 273.7(i)(3)(viii)

Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing August 20, 2008
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.5 Work Requirements 1
heading: 516.5 Work Requirements

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.5 Work Requirements
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.6 Resources and Income
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.6 Resources and Income

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.6 Resources and Income
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
The resource rules in Chapter 540 and income rules in Chapters 550 , 552 , and 555 apply to migrant or seasonal farm worker households. The CAO must count income that is and will be available to the household for the certification period . The following special rules also apply:

The CAO must exempt the value of a licensed vehicle used for long distance travel while the migrant or seasonal farm worker follows the job stream.

7 CFR § 273.8(e)(3)(i)(c)

The CAO must exclude income from an emergency public assistance vendor payment provided to a third party on behalf of the migrant or seasonal farm worker household while the household is in the job stream. This may include emergency payments for housing or transportation.

7 CFR § 273.9(c)(1)

The CAO must not apply the rules of Chapter 552 for averaging contract or self-employment income over a 12-month period, even if the migrant or seasonal farm worker's annual income is earned over a period shorter than a year.

7 CFR § 273.10(c)(3)(ii)

Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing March 26, 2004

body:
The CAO must enroll in the semiannual reporting system a household in which all members are migrant or seasonal farm workers.

Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing January 12, 2006

heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.8 Certification Periods

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.8 Certification Periods
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
Migrant or seasonal farm workers are assigned a six-month certification period . (See Section 575.22 .)

7 CFR § 273.10(f)(3)(iii)

If the migrant or seasonal farm worker household applies after the 15th of the month and is found eligible for expedited benefits, the CAO must certify eligibility for at least the month of application and the following month.

7 CFR § 273.2(i)(4)(iii)(B)

Pending in-state verification must be provided before the CAO can authorize the second month benefit. This may also apply to a third month if the CAO is waiting for out-of-state verification.

Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing August 20, 2008
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.8 Certification Periods 1
heading: 516.8 Certification Periods

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.8 Certification Periods
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.9 Renewal
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.9 Renewal

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.9 Renewal
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
The destitution policy and procedures in Section 516.2 apply at renewal, but only for the first month of each certification period .

At renewal, the CAO must not count income from a new source in the first month of the new certification period if the household will receive less than $25 in income from this new source by the 10th calendar day after the household’s normal issue date.

7 CFR § 273.10(e)(3)(vii)

Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing March 26, 2004
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.9 Renewal 1
heading: 516.9 Renewal

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.9 Renewal
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.10 Proration
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.10 Proration

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 516.10 Proration
metadata.toc_parent: Chapter 516: Migrant or Seasonal Farm Workers

body:
Migrant or seasonal farm worker households are eligible to receive a full monthly benefit for the application month if the household has participated in Pennsylvania or another state in the 30 days before the application month.

The CAO must data-enter an open date equal to the first day of the application month. The system accepts an opening transaction only if the “application received date” on the PA 601 is on or before the open date.

Example: A migrant farm worker household arrives in Pennsylvania on July 28, files an application on July 29, and is found eligible. The household reports receipt of SNAP benefits in North Carolina in early June for the month of June only. Since the household was a participating household within the last 30 days, the CAO must authorize full benefits for July, with an open date and “application received date” of July 1.

7 CFR § 273.10(a)(1)(i)

Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing August 20, 2008

Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) Handbook

Chapter 520: Identity
citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 520: Identity 1
heading: 520 Title

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: Chapter 520: Identity

citation_label: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 520.1 General Policy
heading: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 520.1 General Policy

metadata.title: Pennsylvania SNAP Handbook: 520.1 General Policy

body:
Verification Requirements for Updating a Client’s Name and Gender in eCIS , PFS-19353-520 (July 11, 2019)

The individual completing the application must verify their identity. If an AR applies for the household , the CAO must verify the identity of both the AR and the payment name.

Identity may be verified by readily available documents or by collateral contact . The CAO must accept any document that reasonably proves identity. No specific type of document, such as a birth certificate, is required.

NOTE: The CAO will enter into eCIS the gender that is specified on identity verification.

The CAO must record in the Client Information System ( eCIS ) how the individual verified their identity. Acceptable verification may include, but is not limited to, the following:

Birth certificate.

NOTE: Birth certificates issued from Puerto Rico prior to July 1, 2010 are no longer acceptable and the CAO must assist any household in exploring and obtaining alternative sources of identity verification.

Social Security card.

Driver’s license.

Pay stubs.

Selective Service card.

Voter registration card.

Student identification card.

Employee identification card.

Library card.

Passport.

Mail delivered by the U.S. Postal Service.

State ID issued in place of a driver’s license.

ID issued by a public housing authority.

ID issued by a social welfare agency.

Photo ID previously issued by a public welfare agency.

Contact with a third party who can identify the client.

7 CFR § 273.2(f)(1)(vii)

Reissued March 18, 2026, replacing March 1, 2012

Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) Handbook

Chapter 522: Citizen/Noncitizen
